### Jack Kruse — 2017-05-25T06:12:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/we-need-a-where-to-move-to-thread.19529/post-214624>

This request is riduculous. I tested 5 yrs ago and I test my new location every three months and if you do nto test consistently you'll never know what upgrades are going on around. Just because I test 5 yrs ago means nothing in a blue lit RF/microwaved world. It has gone from 1 G five years ago to now 4 G. YOU MUST TEST. I cannot do your push ups for you. That is why there is an EMF boot camp. It is why Scompy guest blog is there. Every member event we talk about the new things we all need to mito hack in our environment.

This is your job. I cannot come to you house and test for you. You have to have skin in the game. My job is to remind you of this.

### brent-next — 2017-05-25T19:33:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/we-need-a-where-to-move-to-thread.19529/post-214685>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=214624)

> This request is riduculous. I tested 5 yrs ago and I test my new location every three months and if you do nto test consistently you'll never know what upgrades are going on around.

Respectfully, my suggestion is only ridiculous if you think that I was asking you to come to my house and test for me. I certainly was not asking for that.

I have a thousand dollars worth of meters and I know to use them due to my engineering degree and my ham radio background. I'm sure that the EMF Boot Camp is an excellent event and this is probably a good place to promote it.

[https://www.jackkruse.com/emfbootcamp/](https://www.jackkruse.com/emfbootcamp/)

What I am asking for is a top level thread similar to the one for cold thermogenesis where your members can exchange details about the locations that are working (or not working) for them.

Yes, I understand the need for re-testing and I understand that formerly good locations will become bad as new antennas are put up. The tech revolution sucks!

You want your members to recover to optimal health and you say that most people need to move. I am one of the few who are willing and able to move.

Right now I am trying to figure out where I should go and it would be very helpful if there was a top level forum on locations that are working for your members and locations that aren't working. Perhaps so few people have moved that there wouldn't be many contributors, I don't know.

This is your party, you call the tunes and I am having a good time here so thanks.

By the way, my profile picture was taken in Puerto Morelos, Mexico which is very close to Playa Del Carmen. Notice that I don't have a tan in that photo as it was taken during my sun avoidance phase.

Now I know how to make better use of the sun!

### caroline — 2017-05-26T06:08:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/we-need-a-where-to-move-to-thread.19529/post-214730>

We are going to be making a move.... And there is just so much we have to consider.

The more I ponder this - I like Sue UK's idea of renting first ....and then if things go South - you can just pick up and move.

As JK says above - things are constantly changing .....and we need to keep up with it all.

I love the idea of a "Tiny" house .....and also a yurt. The tiny house is a hard sell to my guy - but it is moveable!

We have 2 pages of wants, needs and desires.......but if the area isn't right - nothing else will matter.

We have been away with our camper trailer a lot the last 6+ months. We often see folks with a huge RV and towing a small car. I always am amazed at why you would need something so big. We have talked to couples who have sold up their house and property and bought one of these fifth wheelers and are parked at a camp ground..... so they are free to pick up go whenever it suits them. Maybe not so dumb after all!

We not only have to buy smart - price wise ...but also location, location, location, location - and not primarily for the reasons everyone thinks!

a little of our list....
close enuf to walk to the ocean
low density
near national parks
few power lines, no cell towers
at our age we have to think of where a hospital and doctors are - altho we are both very healthy - we can't be stupid about this either.
distance from family as we age
climate - where we will be looking the climate is moderate. We love hot weather and the sun but would both hate the high humidity in Queensland. We can always pick up and follow the sun with our camper anyway.
We both love getting in the ocean in the winter time - magical C/T. Of course our winter is very moderate so it is a no-brainer

Where is optimal for you????

### Sue-UK — 2017-05-26T10:19:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/we-need-a-where-to-move-to-thread.19529/post-214735>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=214730)
>
> Where is optimal for you????

I think I can get optimal where I am in the UK, even if I have to do extraordinary to do it. There are less than 100 people within a one mile radius, there is no street lighting, I don't pick up neighbours wifi, cell towers are pretty sparse, I have hacked my environment, and keep hacking it. I don't see chemtrails, I'm not near an airport or on the usual flight path for planes. I don't see chemtrails. My garden is thriving, protective even, I watch the insects doing their thing, and the bees are fine. The weather is what it is and its always got something to offer, if my dopamine is high enough to see it ... that's my challenge. Even if DH reluctantly agreed to do it, moving abroad means seeing my kids less and missing out on being an important part of my granddaughters life, and I'm Barefoot Nana ....  My compromise is 2 or 3 separate weeks in the sun over the winter. I think the secret to surviving the higher latitudes could be the skin surface and the fat cell as a storage for light, and a light amplifier, so that's where I'm looking for clues ....

I looked round and I saw a huge cell tower on each of the Gili Islands and the population density with all the tourists and their devices plus the wifi in EVERY hotel and hostel meant that the place has become a hell hole for disease and is definitely NOT optimal... such a shame... go back 30 years and it would have been amazing... Especially for Australians on the West coast who are a 4 hour flight away.

### Sean Waters — 2017-05-29T20:30:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/we-need-a-where-to-move-to-thread.19529/post-215016>

I have a spreadsheet on my laptop, will upload tomorrow if anyone wants it? It is basically a list of countries by population density (the top 150 with a total population of less than 5 million).

Few in the tropics worth mentioning.... Belize, Costa Rica and Nicaragua.

This thread has inspired a real change in my thinking since Saturday. I woke up and felt like shit and I just thought I need to move. I haven't got it out my mind since...

I have spoken to my other half and she is keen to do the same... I am going to take a winter break at Xmas in Belize and the tropics and look around for some optimal spots, maybe do some testing... Or even just document how I feel.

sleep and skin and mood are good indicators.

Finding work out there is better as I work in construction, but still few and far between. The cost of living is very low but still you need to work in this day an age no matter where you are.

Getting residency is fine as long as you stay there for 6 months of a 12 month period. They want you to bring something to their community. That's fine as I'd stay for a long time.

No mortgage, kids or dependent on me in the UK, nothing stopping me. Property, food, bills all cheap just need to find permanent work.

Either way, London is great but I can't stay here much longer... I'm Getting the hell outta dodge...

### brent-next — 2017-06-06T15:01:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/we-need-a-where-to-move-to-thread.19529/post-215484>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=214765)
>
> **WHY MEMBERSHIP MATTERS:** There must be a way to reverse every disease since living things are not designed to by nature to be filled with illness as its default state.

As a gold member, I certainly agree with Jack, that membership is worth my time and my money. Thanks Dr. Jack.

Scompy's guest blog is definitely worth reading when you are deciding where to go: [https://www.jackkruse.com/reality-5-scompys-quantum-quiz-mito-hacking-life/](https://www.jackkruse.com/reality-5-scompys-quantum-quiz-mito-hacking-life/) He ended up in Bend oregon which is probably the best you can do in the pacific northwest, but probably not nearly as good as he could have done if he had been willing to move further south.

As I understand it, the UV increasing effects of going up in altitude are smaller than the UV increasing effects of moving closer to the equator. Also many places at high altitude are quite cloudy (fortunately for Scompy, Bend Oregon is quite clear).

Of course you can always move to a sunny place at altitude in the tropics for the strongest earth bound sunshine possible. In general, most places between 4,000 and 5,000 ft. of elevation in the tropics will have very pleasant year round climates. Look for high plateaus, not valleys between mountains.

Here are my thoughts on where to relocate to when you are trying to get to optimal health.

1\. Anyplace in the Mediterranean is going to be way too cloudy and dusty (dust off the Sahara) in the winter. All of Europe is too cloudy in the winter and much of it is too cloudy in the summer. The Canary islands off Africa have high dust of the Sahara much over them, much of the time.
2\. All mountain and most high hills have antennas on them. If you want to move to a place with mountains, you will have to use your meters to try to find a dead zone that has less non native EMF. The Yucutan peninsula and Belize are notably flat so it should be easier to find dead zones.
3\. If you can see ships on the sea, their radars can see you and will be dosing your mitochondria with radar pulses. Ships pass slowly, so this is a important. Most pleasure boats longer than 30 feet also have marine radar.
4\. If you can see airplanes taking off and landing, you will be getting significant microwave doses.
5\. Some very out of the way places have big radar installations. The Island of Kauai, for example has a very large US Navy radar installation.
6\. Some countries have started to use microwaves much more than others. In Northern Cyprus all the internet was delivered via microwaves from mountains down the middle of the island. In Ukraine, hotels have motion sensor lights in addition to Wi-Fi pounding on your mitochondria.
7\. Vacation to a place you think will work for you, don't move. When you do move, rent first don't buy.
8\. If the sky at night where you plan to stay is not very dark, you are too close to a city. Black night skies mean less visible light pollution and less non native emf pollution.
9\. Ordinary things like crime and culture are also important. Mexico is not the safest place in the best of times and if the price of oil drops 20 dollars per barrel, or if politics reduces the ability of Mexicans living in the United States to send money home, I would expect gringoes living in Mexico to be blamed and the crime situation to get worse.

My current short list of places to go includes northern Argentina, northern Chile and Costa Rica. Anyone been to Arica Chile, Salta Argentina, or the high lands of Costa Rica? Asia might have some good options, but I know nothing about it.

### Sean Waters — 2017-06-06T15:23:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/we-need-a-where-to-move-to-thread.19529/post-215485>

That's a really good summary.

From my little scanning of central America and around: **Belize, Costa Rica, Ecuador and Yucatan Mexico** look the most favourable. I need to look at **Northern Chile and Argentina.**

1.  I have found a volunteer position in Costa Rica, on an island off the coast, with no electricity. I might spend a few months here next year, see how I feel, do some testing, and also explore Costa Rica, Belize and these other countries for a few months.
2.  I'll be note taking and testing in each place - _**and using that summary and other advice on this blog**_ - to reach conclusions on places.
3.  On top of that, I'll be finding suitable Work, Accommodation and Community.
4.  Rent not Buy is Key.

**Asia**

1.  I've travelled there, and most of **Thailand** I felt sick and had an upset stomach - it's blue lit and very electric to accommodate the mass tourism (backpackers).
2.  **Vietnam....** Cities = same as Thailand. Coast/mountain towns = a lot better.
3.  Never went to **Laos or Cambodia** or **Philippines** but heard some areas of these countries are very remote (hopefully low nnEMF),
4.  On the whole, Asia (south east) is beautiful and they want English Speaking professionals to work in their industries. I know a colleague who lived in Cambodia, I have a friend that teaches there - he loves it and has integrated well.

Argentina certainly has miles of beaches, but the area from Salta Argentina north to the Bolivian border is inland. Salta Argentina is at 3,500 ft of altitude. The city of Salta itself will the the typical EMF hell hole, but it should be possible to find a dead zone that still has good all day sun.

Here are some pictures:

[https://www.google.com.ua/search?q=...NzKvUAhXhF5oKHYQ2CdAQ\_AUICigB&biw=845&bih=451](https://www.google.com.ua/search?q=salta+argentina&source=lnms&tbm=isch&sa=X&ved=0ahUKEwjd9NaNzKvUAhXhF5oKHYQ2CdAQ_AUICigB&biw=845&bih=451)

### Lahelada — 2017-06-07T20:29:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/we-need-a-where-to-move-to-thread.19529/post-215575>

Beaches as you already found out are not to be found up there. The beaches in Argentina are cold beaches , three months of the year they are warm and the rest of the year is CT time . If you want warm or tropical beaches you are better off in Brasil or the north of Uruguay.

My choice of Patagonia ; I looked at the North and decided that fro living I did not want to live in an arid ,dry and that hot a climate. After the summers in Buenos Aires iIhanker for a winter so that is why I am looking at Patagonia not the far north but on the 37th latitude. UV is pretty strong in summer ,it gets pretty hot at times and as I am a Northern European haplotype I think it will suit me better long term. I will visit you though to tank UV in the winter .....

EMF : You can look up the whole of Argentina on wigle.net and opensignal.com. Mountains here are not necessarily antenna spots. Too many of them and ones too high up to have one each ! If you go into a built up neigbourhood you need to take street meter readings. Some streets are aerial cable salads and remarkably sane and others with little chaos go off the scale. This goes for all of Latin America really.

Water: Municipal water is technically fluoridated but if they do not have the money ... The bigger problem here is arsenic. You might be over the moon tha you have a well on your land but unless they are deep enough they may have naturally occurring arsenic.

Endemic diseases: Leishmaniasis and lepra are still endemic in the north, Chaga infections too.

Those red, black, yellow sneaks are on the road ran over by cars.
Also tarantulas and other snakes.

### brent-next — 2017-06-09T20:57:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/we-need-a-where-to-move-to-thread.19529/post-215706>

The biological risks to travelers are often wildly higher than the risks of violence to travelers, even when you are traveling to do something like fight a war.

"Influenza and pneumonia killed more American soldiers and sailors during the war \[WW-1\] than did enemy weapons." From this page: [https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2862337/](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2862337/)

These biological risks are hard to evaluate from a distance and they are very important. I don't know where I got Toxoplasma Gondii and I don't know where I got ascaris lumbroicoides, but I got them. I identifed these two infections by going to a lab and paying for every parasite test that they had, which I am sure was only a short list of the parasites that were problems in that particular area.

This post is called, "Your First Post to Read Here at Jackkruse.com" and it's worth re-reading when trying to plan a move:

[https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/your-first-post-to-read-here-at-jackkruse-com.17829/](https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/your-first-post-to-read-here-at-jackkruse-com.17829/)
.
The town of San Pedro de Atacama is 5,000 people at almost 8,000 Ft. with at least a few nice hotels:

[https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/San\_Pedro\_de\_Atacama](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/San_Pedro_de_Atacama)

Or how about staying at this Oasis in the Peruvian part of the Atacama desert (closer to the equator).

[http://www.dailymail.co.uk/travel/t...l-oasis-town-exists-middle-barren-desert.html](http://www.dailymail.co.uk/travel/travel_news/article-2966798/The-mystical-oasis-town-exists-middle-barren-desert.html)

Then there is this resort.

[https://blog.glamping.com/awasi-the-atacama-desert-oasis/](https://blog.glamping.com/awasi-the-atacama-desert-oasis/)

Since intense sunshine seems to be a key part of the healing game, spending time at altitude near the equator in a desert (with one toe in the pool) might be worth considering.

Locations in Chile with good road connections to the Pacific coast (which is not far) should be able to get good seafood from the cold pacific ocean.

But before Nuclear Power Plant started to be build they build hotels, residences and restaurants for American and other workers.
Not everybody was allowed everywhere.
People had tags. 1, 2 & 3.
We had tags with #1, allowed everywhere.
Our house had a separate quarters for maids.
Compound was fenced and guarded.

Every so often jeweler would visit with few suitcases of jewelry and platoon of body guards,
or she would arrange party in her (fortified) residence.

Locals were getting paid weekly.
Immediately changed their not immediately need cruiseros to $$.
We gladly exchanged money with them.
Inflation was rampant, from day to day prices were raised.

...

> As a gold member, I certainly agree with Jack, that membership is worth my time and my money. Thanks Dr. Jack.
>
> Scompy's guest blog is definitely worth reading when you are deciding where to go: [https://www.jackkruse.com/reality-5-scompys-quantum-quiz-mito-hacking-life/](https://www.jackkruse.com/reality-5-scompys-quantum-quiz-mito-hacking-life/) He ended up in Bend oregon which is probably the best you can do in the pacific northwest, but probably not nearly as good as he could have done if he had been willing to move further south.
>
> As I understand it, the UV increasing effects of going up in altitude are smaller than the UV increasing effects of moving closer to the equator. Also many places at high altitude are quite cloudy (fortunately for Scompy, Bend Oregon is quite clear).
>
> Of course you can always move to a sunny place at altitude in the tropics for the strongest earth bound sunshine possible. In general, most places between 4,000 and 5,000 ft. of elevation in the tropics will have very pleasant year round climates. Look for high plateaus, not valleys between mountains.
>
> Here are my thoughts on where to relocate to when you are trying to get to optimal health.
>
> 1\. Anyplace in the Mediterranean is going to be way too cloudy and dusty (dust off the Sahara) in the winter. All of Europe is too cloudy in the winter and much of it is too cloudy in the summer. The Canary islands off Africa have high dust of the Sahara much over them, much of the time.
> 2\. All mountain and most high hills have antennas on them. If you want to move to a place with mountains, you will have to use your meters to try to find a dead zone that has less non native EMF. The Yucutan peninsula and Belize are notably flat so it should be easier to find dead zones.
> 3\. If you can see ships on the sea, their radars can see you and will be dosing your mitochondria with radar pulses. Ships pass slowly, so this is a important. Most pleasure boats longer than 30 feet also have marine radar.
> 4\. If you can see airplanes taking off and landing, you will be getting significant microwave doses.
> 5\. Some very out of the way places have big radar installations. The Island of Kauai, for example has a very large US Navy radar installation.
> 6\. Some countries have started to use microwaves much more than others. In Northern Cyprus all the internet was delivered via microwaves from mountains down the middle of the island. In Ukraine, hotels have motion sensor lights in addition to Wi-Fi pounding on your mitochondria.
> 7\. Vacation to a place you think will work for you, don't move. When you do move, rent first don't buy.
> 8\. If the sky at night where you plan to stay is not very dark, you are too close to a city. Black night skies mean less visible light pollution and less non native emf pollution.
> 9\. Ordinary things like crime and culture are also important. Mexico is not the safest place in the best of times and if the price of oil drops 20 dollars per barrel, or if politics reduces the ability of Mexicans living in the United States to send money home, I would expect gringoes living in Mexico to be blamed and the crime situation to get worse.
>
> My current short list of places to go includes northern Argentina, northern Chile and Costa Rica. Anyone been to Arica Chile, Salta Argentina, or the high lands of Costa Rica? Asia might have some good options, but I know nothing about it.

Great post! Thank you!

We are moving from Glasgow, Scotland to Nerja, Spain in September.

Whilst you are saying it's cloudy it's still a whole load better than where we are now.

We'll be in a town for a few months but it's my plan to check out villages up the mountains for a dead zone!

Really, I want to find the best part of Europe. Greek islands may be a possibility...

### Paul Keenan — 2017-06-30T16:09:13+0300

### brent-next — 2017-07-04T23:33:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/we-need-a-where-to-move-to-thread.19529/post-217969>

> [ScottishEmma said:](/goto/post?id=216526)
>
> We are moving from Glasgow, Scotland to Nerja, Spain in September.
>
> Whilst you are saying it's cloudy it's still a whole load better than where we are now.
> ...
> Really, I want to find the best part of Europe. Greek islands may be a possibility...

Nerja Spain or any of the southern Greek islands for the winter will certainly be better than Glasgow Scotland for the winter. You probably won't get the flue in either place, because most people there don't get the flue in winter.

Whether Spain will be optimal for you or not depends on what you are trying to do. Since you are active on this forum, I am guessing that you are are trying to cure up some chronic condition. I'm guessing that Spain wintertime sunshine will NOT be strong enough to help you get over some chronic condition.

You are probably trying to optimize on several things, like staying in European time zones. My suggestion would be to only optimize on your health and find some place that works 100% well for you for your health. If you get healthy you can always do more things later.

The Canary Islands are the furthest south place that's technically still Europe. The problem is that they can get a lot of dust that's reduces the benefit of the sunshine.

[https://www.google.com/search?q=dus...tvDUAhVJwj4KHS4XARAQ\_AUICigB&biw=1135&bih=615](https://www.google.com/search?q=dust+over+the+Canary+islands&source=lnms&tbm=isch&sa=X&ved=0ahUKEwiw4dWHtvDUAhVJwj4KHS4XARAQ_AUICigB&biw=1135&bih=615)

The Cape Verde islands have the same problem:

[https://www.google.com/search?q=dus...5#tbm=isch&q=dust+over+the+cape+verde+islands](https://www.google.com/search?q=dust+over+the+Canary+islands&source=lnms&tbm=isch&sa=X&ved=0ahUKEwiw4dWHtvDUAhVJwj4KHS4XARAQ_AUICigB&biw=1135&bih=615#tbm=isch&q=dust+over+the+cape+verde+islands)

That leaves you with options in Asia and the Americas. I met a man recently who had been to every country in the world. He recommended Sri Lanka for what I was looking for. You might check it out. I don't know anything about it.

I'm now in Costa Rica which hopefully will work for me. Costa Rica is overall swimming in microwaves so finding a dead zone is difficult but possible.

You also need to consider other things like mosquitoes, snakes and winding mountain roads as significant risks to travelers. In Costa rica, there are a lot of roads on the map or on your GPS that are bad ideas during the day and terrible ideas at night or when it's raining. Most major roads here are paved well, but often they have no reflectors or reflective paint on them to make them safe after dark.

The shoulders next to the roads here are usually washed out, so you definitely need a high clearance vehicle (think SUV - thanks Jack). Also don't drive away from your airport in Costa Rica without a good GPS with a Costa Rican database. Road signs only exist in about 25% of the places that need them. Always do a sanity check here on your GPS route because your GPS will cheerfully send you down roads that are really bad ideas.

Snakes and ordinary crime in Costa Rica are low risks overall I think, but you need to be aware. In some places the locals put up 12 foot tall fences with razor wire on top (Liberia in the north), and in other places they don't (for example Palmes in the western part of the central valley.)

There are many parts of Costa Rica where I wouldn't go because of mosquitoes.

I'm sorry to tell you that Europe just doesn't have any good winter time places with strong sunshine. You can certainly do better than Glasgow in the winter, but just doing better, won't get you to optimal.

I appreciate your comments, truly, you know your stuff!!

I'm not chronically ill at all. I'm in excellent health but I'm active here because I want to live optimally. I realise that long-term the impact of living in a low quantum yield in Scotland will not allow me nor my family to truly thrive.

Europe is where we are at for now. In all honesty I don't relish the prospect of living in Asia or the Americas for a multitude of reasons - some of which you have mentioned on top of logistics.

I will see how we get on with Southern Spain and consider Greece as another option perhaps for next year to test the water (pun!) That is perhaps also dependent on the outcome of Brexit on my ability to move easily.

Thank you for your input.

### OptimalSkr — 2017-07-05T21:27:51+0300

### brent-next — 2017-07-06T00:00:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/we-need-a-where-to-move-to-thread.19529/post-218038>

> [OptimalSkr said:](/goto/post?id=218030)
>
> Have you ruled out all places in the USA as optimal?

I'm in Costa Rica now at 10 degrees north and about 3,500 ft. elevation. I could go anywhere and that's what I have done.

You need to figure out what will be right for you. It's the rainy season in Costa Rica now (until December), with not as much sun as I would like. I probably will be able to get enough sun for me, but not every place in Costa Rica is sunny now and some places are always cloudy.

It's much easier to find a good place for six months of the year than to find a place that is good all year.

### brent-next — 2017-07-16T19:01:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/we-need-a-where-to-move-to-thread.19529/post-219060>

2017 is not shaping up to be a sunny year in Costa Rica. Many long time residents agree that this is a particularly rainy year.

[http://www.expat.com/forum/viewtopic.php?id=690453](http://www.expat.com/forum/viewtopic.php?id=690453)

So if you are looking for a place to become optimal, Cost Rica before December 2017 is probably not your best bet. In December the dry season starts (it runs through the end of April) and in those months, you are very likely to get enough sun here.

After having been here in Costa Rica for three weeks, my advice for now would be, "Go somewhere else that is likely to be sunnier this year." The north Pacific coast of Costa Rica near the Liberia international airport, in the Guanacaste province is indeed sunnier, than several places I have been, but they also have a lot more mosquitoes than the central valley does.

### brent-next — 2017-07-17T01:55:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/we-need-a-where-to-move-to-thread.19529/post-219103>

Here's the forecast for Playa del Carmen Mexico:

[https://www.google.com/search?q=pla...rome..69i57.5165j0j7&sourceid=chrome&ie=UTF-8](https://www.google.com/search?q=playa+del+carmen+weather&oq=playa+del+carmen+weather&aqs=chrome..69i57.5165j0j7&sourceid=chrome&ie=UTF-8)

Now here's the forecast for where I am in San Jose, Cost Rica.

[https://www.google.com/search?q=san...rome..69i57.6854j0j7&sourceid=chrome&ie=UTF-8](https://www.google.com/search?q=san+jose+costa+rica+forcast&oq=san+jose+costa+rica+forcast&aqs=chrome..69i57.6854j0j7&sourceid=chrome&ie=UTF-8)

The daily forecast for thunderstorms in Costa Rica doesn't tell the full story of the San Jose Costa Rica weather. In most years it means "strong morning sun -- then afternoon thunderstorms" for a very comfortable climate. We've gotten a few days of that but most of the time I've been here it has been 80 to 90% cloudy all day.

For getting consistent daily sun, Playa del Carmen looks like a much better choice this year.

I think the big lesson here is to only optimize on one thing - getting healthy. If you get really healthy you have bought time and can always do more things later.

Trying to optimize on more than one thing is really difficult, and you are likely to get it wrong. In my case, I am trying to find a good place for me to get healthy AND a good place for my sweetheart to have a baby. We certainly have gotten some intense sun in Costa Rica, just not as many hours of it as I would have liked.

Hi Brent - thanks for these updates. Really insightful and useful for everyone on this forum - as I think it is the end goal for most of us.

I have a relocation job opportunity to Costa Rica and was considering it, but likely not to move as I want to stop working in an office. But I may have to do it for the income at some opportunity, and there is a big Marina being constructed there.

Do you know anything about Belize and Guatemala?

### brent-next — 2017-07-18T23:13:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/we-need-a-where-to-move-to-thread.19529/post-219313>

> [Sean Waters said:](/goto/post?id=219240)
>
> I have a relocation job opportunity to Costa Rica and was considering it, but likely not to move as I want to stop working in an office. But I may have to do it for the income at some opportunity, and there is a big Marina being constructed there.
>
> Do you know anything about Belize and Guatemala?

Whether you should take a job in Costa Rica or not depends on what your alternative course of action would be. Where do you live now and what will you do if you don't take the job here? If your alternative course of action is to stay in an office job in the UK, then Costa Rica may be a better choice.

All Costa Rican cites and small towns are EMF hell holes, but then so are all UK cities.

Here you will be able to get strong sunshine on weekends, and lunch breaks (some of the time) and in the UK, you essentially never will.

If you are a valuable enough potential employee, your company will pay for you to fly to Cost Rica and see what it is like. I highly recommend that you ask for such a trip and take it if they offer to pay for it. Costa Rica is a place of stunning natural beauty and very friendly people.

I spent two months in Antiqua Guatemala a few years ago and Guatemala is also a place of stunning natural beauty, (check out these pictures of lake Atitlan).

[https://www.google.com/search?q=lak...y5PVAhXH6iYKHRo7BoAQ\_AUIBygC&biw=1135&bih=575](https://www.google.com/search?q=lake+atitlan&source=lnms&tbm=isch&sa=X&ved=0ahUKEwidp-eFy5PVAhXH6iYKHRo7BoAQ_AUIBygC&biw=1135&bih=575)

I don't want to go back to Guatemala for several reasons. 1. Guatemala today is owned and run by the descendants of the original Spanish invaders who took over Guatemala 500 years ago. They fly around in private helicopters (really) while the common people ride around in used American school busses.

Read about the Guatemalan civil war before you go there.

[https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Guatemalan\_Civil\_War](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Guatemalan_Civil_War)

This was was essentially a re-fighting of the Spanish wars of conquest, and the indigenous people once again lost.

At the time of my visit, it was safe for tourists to stay in any town, but a little bit out of town, it was not safe.

Belize is flat and visually uninteresting place unless you are scuba diving or fishing. It's also a very high crime location including crime against tourists:

[http://abc7chicago.com/news/dangers-in-paradise-what-tourists-arent-being-told/1189365/](http://abc7chicago.com/news/dangers-in-paradise-what-tourists-arent-being-told/1189365/)

[https://www.osac.gov/pages/ContentReportDetails.aspx?cid=21487](https://www.osac.gov/pages/ContentReportDetails.aspx?cid=21487)

In Costa Rica, stealing your stuff is a national past time. Twelve foot high fences with razor wire on top are standard in many parts of the country. Still -- I think your big risk in Costa Rica is not crime, it's mosquitoes and dangerous mountain roads. Don't drive at night (or in the rain) here and don't assume that every road on your map or GPS is safe to drive on.

Remember that it's summer time in the UK now and your dopamine is relatively high. That means you can make the decision to relocate now while in January, you are likely to be so low on dopamine that you won't be able to decide to go anywhere.

### Sean Waters — 2017-07-20T11:39:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/we-need-a-where-to-move-to-thread.19529/post-219479>

My alternative course of action, or at least plan of action, is:

\- save up each month in UK for next 12-18 months and have enough money to travel central America and find a spot (using EMF RF detector etc) where I can put a deposit down and set up a guesthouse/hostel that is Technology free and reduced EMF. A dreamy spot would be near the beach in a quiet place with decent transport links - I realise I'm asking for a lot here, but why the hell not.

OR...
\- Do the above but instead of the hostel idea I'd just spend my time volunteering and travelling and then when I run out of money come back to UK to save up again and then go back.

Problem is... I don't have that much time to wait and save - my health is declining and each day I get more frustrated with the lack of energy and poor sleep I have in London. It's affecting my relationships and I'm spending a huge amount of money on seafood and ice to CT each night, which is effectively reducing the amount I'm saving. My Dopamine is extremely low right now... My tendency towards booze, cigarettes and sugary food is at it's highest and I'm constantly hungry. This also, is reducing the amount I'm saving too.

The job in Costa Rica sounds like it would just be moving my problems in the UK to another country... I'd be put up in an apartment with high EMF, forced to use a car and work in an Office. The other thing is, it's just an opportunity I've seen, it's not confirmed I'll get it or anything.

All of these countries sound pretty bad in terms of Crime, but are they as bad as London? Still, in London the police are probably a lot better so maybe I wouldn't want to take my kids to a country like those...

What about the other countries, Nicaragua? Mexico? Honduras?... any experience of these?

If Central America isn't the right move, then where is?... South America? Any places there that you would recommend?

I could move to Spain pretty easily, and I could move to New Zealand even quicker... But again I would need to work in an Office as the cost of living is so high.

### Sue-UK — 2017-07-21T13:48:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/we-need-a-where-to-move-to-thread.19529/post-219598>

I've met retired Brits in Spain who I think made the decision to move with low dopamine, who've then given up because they couldn't recreate what they had at home (same) but with added sun. Younger people can probably wing it easier, or be flexible about moving on somewhere else. My brother lives in Spain, he started learning Spanish 6 months before he went, continued for a couple of years when he got there, through a language school, and also did/does self study. He started off with basic Spanish but he is now fluent enough to handle any social, business or crisis situation in the Spanish language. He eats and shops where the Spanish do, lives in a Spanish village, so avoids the tourists prices. He's a resident, not a long term tourist. To him the COL (Costa Living ) is much lower because he's _living_ there, but he knew from the get go that his ability to speak the language was going to be critical to it working out long term. Not only for the financial side, but for the social side it opens up new friendship possibilities, instead of the only thing in common with someone being that you both speak English ....

### brent-next — 2017-07-24T02:51:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/we-need-a-where-to-move-to-thread.19529/post-219875>

> [Sean Waters said:](/goto/post?id=219479)
>
> find a spot (using EMF RF detector etc) where I can put a deposit down and set up a guesthouse/hostel that is Technology free and reduced EMF.
>
> If Central America isn't the right move, then where is?... South America? Any places there that you would recommend?
>
> I could move to Spain pretty easily, and I could move to New Zealand even quicker... But again I would need to work in an Office as the cost of living is so high.

If you find a place that is technology free with reduced EMF, mark me down as your first paying customer.

Try to optimize on as few things as possible. If your health is declining, just optimize on your health, not your career or doing what is convenient. If you get healthier you have bought more time and you can make more money later.

Let me comment on some of your ideas. I've lived in New Zealand long enough to become a Kiwi resident, so for getting healthy it's definitely NOT a good place all year. Most of the country is quite rainy for all months except December through March. New Zealand only has good weather from December until March.

While you are only optimizing on one thing also don't try to find a place that is perfect all year. That's much too difficult. Find a place that is good for the time you will be there then move.

Spain, Portugal, Greece and Cyprus are all much sunnier places in the summer than the UK. These are also better choices than the UK or Germany for the winter, but the sun won't be strong enough to get you optimal in the winter.

Cost Rica might be an excellent choice between November and April. The central valley stays green and the climate is perfect between 3,500 and 4,500 ft. elevation. It should be possible to find some locations without cell phone service.

Hawaii may have some places that would be good. I lived on Kauai for a couple of years and I don't recommend it due to the big Navy Radar installation there:

[https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific\_Missile\_Range\_Facility](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Missile_Range_Facility)

You might look for something on Maui or the Big Island. Remember that all of Hawaii can be affected by volcanic smoke, even Kauai, which is 200 miles away from the active volcano.

People focus on the risk of crime and tend to forget the risk of death by drowning.

Costa Rica beaches have very dangerous rip tides that are rarely marked.

[http://www.ticotimes.net/2015/06/12/drownings-costa-rica-spur-experts-call-lifeguards](http://www.ticotimes.net/2015/06/12/drownings-costa-rica-spur-experts-call-lifeguards)

I actually saw three children get caught in a riptide and were unable to get back to shore. Fortunately they were rescued by some strong surfers who paddled out there and brought them safely back to shore.

I'd say if you can easily move to Spain or Portugal, go there now, find a spot with low EMF somewhere near the southern coast and think about where to go in October when the sun will be weak again. Make sure you don't rent a place with a good view of ships on the sea, because if you can see the ships, their radars can see you. Take your meters and test everying.

Playa del Carmen, will have strong sun over the summer unless it has a hurricane. I'm not there in Mexico now because of Mexico's overall high crime rate. The tourist areas are much safer and when you go there you need to stay in the tourist areas and follow local safety advice. The beaches between Cancun and Playa del Carmen are stunningly beautiful and I think much safer (no rip tides) than the beaches in Costa Rica.

Maybe I should have gone to Playa del Carmen, but I spent April to May one year near there and it was very hot and uncomfortable. Now I know that I should spend more time in the Cenotes, which I did not know about on at that time.

Costa Rica overall is NOT cheap. It's at least as expensive for a tourist to visit Costa Rica as a visit to the United States and remember that I'm here in the off season.

Groceries are about twice what you would pay for in the United States etc. It is definitely possible to live very cheaply in Costa Rica, but you need to allow yourself a budget and time to figure out how to do that. Your first month here is likely to be expensive.

The purpose of this site is to talk about how to best get to optimal health. But people have asked what I do for a living in this thread and have talked about how to best learn Spanish.

I make language learning software for a living and I am conversant in both Spanish and Russian. My software is an excellent choice for those who want to go from knowing close to nothing to being conversant in Spanish. [https://language101.com/](https://language101.com/)

I'm guessing that some of the users of this forum will come up with even better suggestions of where to move and or where to take short or long vacations.

### caroline — 2017-07-24T04:00:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/we-need-a-where-to-move-to-thread.19529/post-219884>

There is huge U.S. warship of the coast of Queensland right now - I can't even begin to imagine what that is doing to people there.

We are thinking we will move down the east coast of Australia where there is low population and low light pollution ....and then spend the winter in Queensland.

We are going to buy a small RV and then follow the sun. It has crossed our mind to buy a fifth wheeler and live in that permanently - they are gorgeous - but I can't imagine towing one of those things around all the time. But who knows. Eighteen months ago we decided we didn't want to travel around Australia - but now that is all we can think about.

There is so much to think about. We are becoming more aware of all the beautiful beaches here but there is lots of rips - so not good for swimming.

We do find that our perception of what we want and need is changing rapidly these days - we need more money to keep up!

We definitely want less and don't care about stuff anymore - but we have to get the geography right.

### brent-next — 2017-07-24T20:13:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/we-need-a-where-to-move-to-thread.19529/post-219960>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=219884)
>
> There is huge U.S. warship of the coast of Queensland right now - I can't even begin to imagine what that is doing to people there.
>
> We are thinking we will move down the east coast of Australia where there is low population and low light pollution ....and then spend the winter in Queensland.
>
> We are going to buy a small RV and then follow the sun.

My testing from a resort in Northern Cyprus shows me that your basic contain ship at about a mile off shore produces radar pulses with the same signal strength on my meter as a typical Wi-Fi at 15 feet away.

I think the lesson here is don't ignore the marine, military and aviation EMF pollution, but whatever you do, don't think that your wi-fi and your mobile phone are harmless. I've tested two new Toyotas that have what are essentially always on cell phone transmitters built in.

I think that your following the sun idea has a lot of potential. Before you go, get yourself a small portable RF spectrum analyzer and learn how to use it. These things are made for tech savvy engineers, so expect to take some time to learn how to use it. You want one with as much of the spectrum covered as you can find.

### brent-next — 2017-07-25T19:17:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/we-need-a-where-to-move-to-thread.19529/post-220052>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=220013)
>
> when you have tested the toyotas Brent - is this Blue tooth you are speaking of?

I'm not sure what the source of the signals was. One Toyota was a late model Hilux Pickup, I was a passenger in, and the other was a late model Rav4 that I was renting. The Hilux had a continious signal (that went away when you turned the car off) and the Rav4 produced about four ten second bursts per minute, that also went away when the car was turned off.

This is the meter that I was using: [http://www.emfields-solutions.com/detectors/acoustimeter.asp](http://www.emfields-solutions.com/detectors/acoustimeter.asp) I have found this to be a very useful meter.

The obvious solution to this problem would be to check the documentation for any features like tracking, wi-fi, or bluetooth that might be emitting these signals.

If the car was yours, you could then find which fuse supplied power to that transmitter and pull that fuse. Hopefully it wouldn't also disable other features that you needed.

I also have one of these spectrum analyzers. [http://j3.rf-explorer.com/](http://j3.rf-explorer.com/) This will let you figure out what the source of a signal is without any documentation, but it takes more time, effort and skill. Try to find a local RF engineer and see what he uses and recommends. Expect there to be a learning curve with any spectrum analyzer.

There is one easy to get to place in the United States that is NOT an EMF hell hole and it might be a good choice for some people, some of the time. It's the United States National Radio Quiet Zone and it's located mostly between West Virginia and Virginia.

[https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/United\_States\_National\_Radio\_Quiet\_Zone](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/United_States_National_Radio_Quiet_Zone)

Radio transmissions are very limited in this area. In some areas you can't even use a microwave oven. It's a lot further north than I would like at 38 to 39 degrees, but between some time in May and some time in August, it might be the easiest and best choice that most North Americans can get to.

It's also a naturally very beautiful place. If you can find camping or hiking areas they might be wonderful in the summer: It will be very cloudy in the winter time and much of the spring and fall.

[https://www.google.com/search?q=wea...69i57j69i64.9198j0j4&sourceid=chrome&ie=UTF-8](https://www.google.com/search?q=weather+harrisonburg+virginia.&oq=weather+harrisonburg+virginia.&aqs=chrome..69i57j69i64.9198j0j4&sourceid=chrome&ie=UTF-8)

Within this zone, there are various levels of radio frequency restriction, with towns and anything near a road being worse, and the areas away from towns being better.

The scientists will probably tell you if they are also transmitting, and they may also be able to give you some good suggestions on which type of cars emit less RF.

This image shows a sign that allows only diesels.

[https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Unite...ile:Warning\_signs\_at\_NRAO\_zone\_1\_entrance.jpg](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/United_States_National_Radio_Quiet_Zone#/media/File:Warning_signs_at_NRAO_zone_1_entrance.jpg)

The right diesel is probably your best choice for a low EMF producing vehicle. All older diesels would be fairly low EMF vehicles and only some new ones would have low emissions.

### brent-next — 2017-08-13T19:40:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/we-need-a-where-to-move-to-thread.19529/post-221600>

Some of you, especially Europeans and Canadians, should consider spending more time in Cuba.

The apparently have the best control of mosquitoes of any place in the Carribean.

[https://www.nature.com/news/mosquit...how-cuba-kept-zika-at-bay-for-so-long-1.20429](https://www.nature.com/news/mosquito-guns-and-heavy-fines-how-cuba-kept-zika-at-bay-for-so-long-1.20429)

[https://www.statnews.com/2016/11/08/zika-in-cuba/](https://www.statnews.com/2016/11/08/zika-in-cuba/)

While no one is in love with the chemical sprays they are using, they are better than getting evil mosquito borne diseases.

I haven't been there so I can't report on it personally, but it looks like it has a lot to offer. United States citizens still have travel restrictions on going to Cuba, but US citizens apparently can legally go to Cuba now if they do the right paperwork.

My guess is that there is less wi-fi etc in Cuba, than in most other countries. But that's just a guess.

> [https://www.nature.com/news/mosquit...how-cuba-kept-zika-at-bay-for-so-long-1.20429](https://www.nature.com/news/mosquito-guns-and-heavy-fines-how-cuba-kept-zika-at-bay-for-so-long-1.20429)
>
> [https://www.statnews.com/2016/11/08/zika-in-cuba/](https://www.statnews.com/2016/11/08/zika-in-cuba/)
>
> While no one is in love with the chemical sprays they are using, they are better than getting evil mosquito borne diseases.
>
> I haven't been there so I can't report on it personally, but it looks like it has a lot to offer. United States citizens still have travel restrictions on going to Cuba, but US citizens apparently can legally go to Cuba now if they do the right paperwork.
>
> My guess is that there is less wi-fi etc in Cuba, than in most other countries. But that's just a guess.

This place crossed my mind as a place to move to... but I brushed it aside because I assumed/imagined it would be difficult to actually emigrate and live there. Not sure why I thought that, I think I still associate it as a communist country and that becoming a resident there would be difficult. But that's probably a massively incorrect assumption... I'm glad you pointed it out too, because my Mother visited there recently, she said she felt really really good in Cuba, and when she got back to the UK she was ill for 3 weeks... must have been the bombardment of Wifi and EMF after a week of almost nothing.

I'm going to start researching Cuba...

### brent-next — 2017-08-31T02:25:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/we-need-a-where-to-move-to-thread.19529/post-222618>

> [OptimalSkr said:](/goto/post?id=218030)
>
> Have you ruled out all places in the USA as optimal?

After two months here in Costa Rica (late June to late August) I definitely can say that many places in the USA would have been a Lot better than Costa Rica now. Definitely don't come to Costa Rica in the rainy season. UV disappears on most days about noon with rain and thunderstorms all afternoon most days.

I didn't go to any of them, mostly because I have a Ukrainian girlfriend who can't easily get a USA visa.

Here are locations that I would consider in the USA.

1\. Somewhere close to the Green Bank radio telescope in West Virginia for the mid May to mid August time frame. This location probably has the lowest amount of non-native EMF of any easy to reach, nice place in the world.

Plan on spending as much time outdoors in your tan through swimsuit as you can.

2\. The Florida Keys, any time that a hurricane is not blowing. It's a long string of small islands and some probably have much less non native EMF than Key West which probably has much lower non native EMF than say Miami. The sunshine is intense and it's good almost all day sun.

Hopefully someone with more experience than I have in the Keys can comment on which ones (or what times of the year) might be good for mitochondriacs.

3\. Some places in the US Virgin Islands. Beware of locations with mosquito problems. I've never been there, but I hear good things about St. Thomas.

4\. Some places in Hawaii that are very sunny, notably Maui.

### brent-next — 2017-09-12T02:41:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/we-need-a-where-to-move-to-thread.19529/post-223416>

> [ScottishEmma said:](/goto/post?id=223367)
>
> Any thoughts on Malta?

"Malta is the most densely populated country in the EU and one of the most densely populated countries in the world, with about **1,265 inhabitants per square kilometre**(**3,000 per square mile**). This compares with about 32 per square kilometre (**85 per square mile**) for the United States."

[https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Demographics\_of\_Malta](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Demographics_of_Malta)

That's a LOT of wi-fi's and mobile phones in addition to marine radars.

I spent last winter in Cyprus at about that latitude, and while it was far enough south to keep me from getting the flue, it wasn't far enough south to get any intense, health restoring sun in the winter.

I'm guessing that right now you are looking for a health restoring vacation, not a permanent place to move to or to spend at least 6 months of the year in.

You might start by re-reading this blog by Jack Kruse on how Circadian timing tunes life.

[https://www.jackkruse.com/time-22-basics-circadian-timing-tunes-life/](https://www.jackkruse.com/time-22-basics-circadian-timing-tunes-life/)

Then the next thing you should do is not screw up your circadian biology by flying east or west more than 1 time zone. So if you are starting in Scotland, you have several decent choices depending on what time of year it is. In Summer you could go to Portugal or the Azores, and in winter you should go to the Canary Islands or the Cape Verde islands. I haven't been to any of those places so get some advice from someone who has.

Don't make the mistake that I did of going to a tropical place during it's very cloudy rainy season. Costa Rica is a bad choice between May and November. The Canary Islands do have at least one rainy month and they can get high dust off the Sahara desert that makes the sun much less strong.

East or west vacations are probably okay if you will be staying for two months or more. There may be some fast jet lag adjustment tricks that might help, but my first guess would be that for optimal health restoration on a one week or two vacation, don't fly east or west more than 1 time zone.

### brent-next — 2017-10-01T02:43:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/we-need-a-where-to-move-to-thread.19529/post-224550>

In less than a week, I should have my baby's Costa Rican and US passports and will be able to leave Costa Rica, hopefully for a much sunnier, much lower non-native EMF location with no other biological risks like mosquitoes or sand flies.

October is the most cloudy and rainy month of the year in Costa Rica with November also having a lot of rain. The dry and sunny season in Costa Rica starts some time in December and runs through the end of April.

I am still scratching my head about where to go now. The Yucutan Peninsula or Cuba would be good choices starting about December 1. Right now the chance of getting another hurricane seems quite high.

My current short list is Arica Chile and perhaps going south in Chile as the Chilean spring turns to summer.

Here's a summary of the yearly weather for Arica, Chile

[https://weatherspark.com/y/26550/Average-Weather-in-Arica-Chile-Year-Round](https://weatherspark.com/y/26550/Average-Weather-in-Arica-Chile-Year-Round)

The beaches in northern Peru have warmer temperatures but apparently don't get strong UV due to high thin clouds. Arica may have the same problem.

The beaches in Arica will be cool and sunny in October. It's worth nothing that the cold water off Chile makes a marine fog layer (similar to San Diego's) that wipes out early morning and evening sun. It may be difficult (perhaps impossible) to find a place to stay at the right distance inland to avoid this.

My experience in Costa Rica tells me that I feel a LOT better with some time in UV index of 10 or 11 sun compared to UV index of 5 or 6.

Weather is really important in finding a good place to get optimal. I'm here at 10 degrees north in Costa Rica and usually the maximum UV index of the day is about 6 and it occurs at about 9:00 a.m. So there are lots of really cloudy places in the tropics.

So does anyone have any better suggestions on where to go between October and November? I would prefer to say in the Americas and I can't go to the United States because my baby's mother is Ukrainian and can't easily get a US visa.

### brent-next — 2017-10-11T22:36:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/we-need-a-where-to-move-to-thread.19529/post-225613>

On Saturday I plan to fly to Cancun, be there for a few days then to drive to Merida or Progresso Mexico and look for a good place to spend the winter.

This should be a lot sunnier than Costa Rica, at least until December. It's flat, so there are no mountains with antennas on them. It's flat, so the roads are safer, and it should be possible to find a place to rent where the non-native EMF will be a lot lower than where I am now.

If anyone wants to meet me for lunch near Cancun or Merida, please send me a private message on this forum.

### Jake Tassell — 2017-11-22T14:10:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/we-need-a-where-to-move-to-thread.19529/post-228621>

> [Cameron Page said:](/goto/post?id=228614)
>
> Me and my wife are planning to move to Spain in Canary Island. I was wondering where is the best Island there so we do a little research something about the place and came up with this article here in [https://www.canaryislandsinfo.co.uk/tenerife/places/](https://www.canaryislandsinfo.co.uk/tenerife/places/) .. What do you think guys? Any tips?

Don't know about Tenerife, but I can tell you about Lanzarote....

I've been to Lanzarote a few times. It's a volcanic island with no rivers or streams and very little rainfall. Apart from some areas inland, it's such a tough environment for living things, even the cacti have to be put on life-support systems. The drinking water is of the de-salinated kind, don't know what adulterants it contains. All the supermarkets sell bottled 'Highland Spring' water, which ain't got no fluoride in it.

I had used Lanzarote pretty much as a cheap date with HRH Sol Invictus. It's always been a bit slow-tech, so pretty easy to rent an adobe hacienda with no Wi Fi, but I went back there this October to find the resort areas have had a go-for-the-throat tech upgrade. There's 5G in all the bars and restaurants, so harsh it could probably tear a layer of your skin off in about half an hour, and I had to move apartment 'cause there was a frigging Wi Fi Death Ray transmitter right behind it.

### ScottishEmma — 2017-12-11T10:16:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/we-need-a-where-to-move-to-thread.19529/post-229982>

I've been living in Southern Spain for three months. There are masts everywhere and the WORST light inside all the cafes and bars and shops. Fortunately with the weather we can eat outside all the time and limit exposure to light - many of the streetlamps are yellow rather than white...

We could go further up into the mountain to try and find a dead zone but to be honest we prefer being a little closer to civilization for all kinds of reasons. Personally, the weather and food here has helped me to thrive. The sun matters a lot.

We're in Nerja.

### brent-next — 2017-12-14T00:59:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/we-need-a-where-to-move-to-thread.19529/post-230262>

> [Cameron Page said:](/goto/post?id=228614)
>
> Me and my wife are planning to move to Spain in Canary Island. I was wondering where is the best Island there so we do a little research something about the place and came up with this article here in [https://www.canaryislandsinfo.co.uk/tenerife/places/](https://www.canaryislandsinfo.co.uk/tenerife/places/) .. What do you think guys? Any tips?

A good location in the Canary islands will be good in the summer if there is not too much dust off the Sahara. Do a Google search for "Dust over the Canary Islands" and look at the images. I do not know how much of the year they have this problem.

Also in my opinion 28 degrees north is just too far north in the winter. If there is no dust and you find the right location it could be good in the summer.

Remember that there is no perfect place but some are a LOT better than others. The Canary Islands even in winter might be a lot better than where you are now. What are your other options?

### brent-next — 2017-12-24T23:34:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/we-need-a-where-to-move-to-thread.19529/post-231037>

What do all the native Mexicans who live in the Yucutan peninsula know that you don't?

They know that the right kind of hammocks are better for sleeping than beds. Almost everyone here sleeps in hammocks, and I 100% agree that the local hammocks are better than beds.

If you are planning on visiting Cancun, Playa del Carmen or anywhere in the Yucutan peninsula, you will notice strange T shaped bars coming out of the walls at head high in every hotel room and every house. These are hammock hooks. Sometimes they have a simple steel tab coming out of the wall with a strong steel hook in it.

Every house and hotel room here has these, some houses, even fancy rich ones, don't have any beds in them at all. Everyone who lives here sleeps in hammocks at least much of the time..

I've been sleeping in a hammock for the last six weeks and it like it much better than even the best bed I ever slept on in my life. My favorite thing about my sleeping hammock is that I can sleep uniformly cool over my entire body. In a typical bed, at best, I would be hot on the bottom and cold on top.

If you want to try sleeping in a hammock here, I recommend that you try it for at least 4 or 5 days to get the hang of it. After my first hammock night I was slightly sore, but after several nights I got it dialed in and I like it much better than any bed.

My sweetheart also reports dramatically reduced snoring which is another plus.

When you come here as a tourist, there are penty of people who will try to sell you overpriced and colorful hammocks, with a story about how it took their mother two weeks to make it by hand. Do NOT buy one of these. These will have very large holes between the strings when in use and will be uncomfortable to sleep in.

Find a Mexican guide, who has been sleeping in a hammock since his parents conceived him in one and go with him to the market to buy your hammock. You want to find a hammock with threads that are 1/8" to 1/4" apart when the hammock is in use. Do not buy one of these without local help, or you will make a bad decision. You also need to buy extension ropes for each end as not every room has the hammock hooks set the same distance apart.

There are many different sizes of hammocks. I'm using a size called "matrimonial" which is big enough for two people. I find this very comfortable.

Have your Mexican guide show you how to safely tie the extension ropes and how to use it. You can tie it higher off the ground for a flatter spine and lower for more curve. You can sleep in a lot of different positions in these hammocks. I like to sleep at an angle, so my body is NOT lined up with the long axis of the hammock.

I know at least one other member of this site who sleeps in a hammock all the time when he is here.

### brent-next — 2017-12-31T00:44:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/we-need-a-where-to-move-to-thread.19529/post-231322>

I will be at the Optimal Resolution conference in Playa Del Carmen on January 3 and 4. If anyone wants to meet me there, please send me a private message with your e-mail.

I thought I would give my report on the Progresso Mexico area for anyone who is interested.

Here is my list of the positives and negatives.

Positives.

1\. Low crime. People leave valuable things locked in cars and no one bothers them. Fences around houses are decorative and functional. Very few people here build 12 foot tall fences here with razor wire to prevent crime. Overall the Mexican state of Yucutan is quite safe.

2\. Fish. Lots of fish in the Gulf of Mexico. Many times I have bought fresh fish and been eating them within an hour of when they were caught.

3\. Price. You can rent basic (not fancy) beach style Mexican houses on the beach starting at $500 per month. Asking prices are often three times that, but you should start low with offers and expect to go up some. Typical hotel prices in Merida are 1/3 of the prices in Cancun.

4\. Lots of empty housing here in from September till March. The beach houses here are built for and owned by rich Mexicans mostly from Merida. They come here during July and August, and again for two weeks during holy week and easter. Most houses that are empty could be rented even if they don't have a for rent sign up. The trick is to find the local maintenance man and ask him to call the owner.

5\. Short cheap flights to the USA.

6\. Same time zone as Chicago.

7\. Good internet in some houses. My current house has fiber optic internet. However don't assume that new houses can be connected even if the fiber optic line is in front of your house. If the house you want doesn't already have good internet, don't rent it.

8\. Fairly sunny weather in the winter.

Negatives:

1\. Mosquitoes. They have tiny silent mosquitoes here that can carry zika, denge, and chikungunya virus. Even if you don't get one of those diseases (which I don't think I have gotten) the local mosquitoes can give you infected mosquito bites which itch like crazy and will become badly infected if not treated. The tratement here is a local antibiotic and retinol salve called Vitacilina. If you get mosquito bites that don't go away quickly you should immediately treat them with this salve.

2\. Mangrove swamps. Mangrove swamps naturally stink like the devil. Here during the daytime, the wind is off the gulf and is fresh. At night it becomes calm and reverses to be off the stinky hydrogen sulfide (H2S) mangrove swamps and you can wake up to a house that smells like a sewer.

3\. Magnetism? Progresso is close to the center of the famous chixilub crater. If my reading of this Mexican authored (in English) research paper is correct, the increase in magnetism here ranges from +506 nano teslas to - 877 nano teslas on an overall background of 40,000 NT in this area overall.

There are other good reasons to come here, but don't come here for the high magnetic field, because it really isn't that significant.

4\. Sunshine is not that intense. The sunshine here on December 10 read a maximum of 5 on my Sun Friend UV meter. That's a LOT less intense than the typical readings of 10 in Costa Rica when I left in October. During the northern hemisphere winter, maybe it's good to simply not be in the northern hemisphere.

On December 21, the shadows at solar noon here were still about as long as the item casting them. I could have been fairly far south in Chile or Argentina at this time of year and had more intense sun.

5\. Unpleasantly strong winds off the gulf. The winds can often be too strong for comfortable sunbathing even if it's sunny.

6\. Not as sunny as I would like. There are often a lot of contrails from jets that become clouds. It's still a mostly sunny place, but high thin clouds can make your sunshine less intense than you might like.

Is it a good choice for you? For many of you it would be a lot better than where you are now. It's not perfect, but it's the relative goodness compared to where you are now that counts.

After my two days at the Optimal Resolution even in Playa del Carmen, I plan to spend at least a week or two on the island of Isla Mujeres which is 6 miles from Cancun. The winds are said to be lighter and there is said to be less stink and in some places no stink from the mangrove swamps.

Costa Rica, between December and April, would have much more intense sunshine.

### Scompy — 2018-02-18T23:02:58+0200

### Lahelada — 2018-03-19T12:23:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/we-need-a-where-to-move-to-thread.19529/post-237506>

I think the hours of sunshine are misleading.You need to know the UVB availabilty hours. So once you have the place you need to calculate your time in which you can make UVB. Haplotype is not the only determining factor and European haplotype is a bit too general. You also nneed to take into account what you want to cure/improve. Some things will not respond to anything less than equatorial sun. Dr K. has said in another thread that Europe is insufficient. ( I am not conversant with your details but making this as applicable to as many as possible)

Have you considered Eilat in Israel? There are decent cheap flights with Ryanair and you get a three months visa.

On your list I think only the Costa del Sol ,Malaga etc makes the grade. Dont forget to think about EMF ,too.

### Michalis — 2018-03-19T22:57:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/we-need-a-where-to-move-to-thread.19529/post-237554>

> [Neil Taylor said:](/goto/post?id=237543)
>
> Open to anywhere. I’ve tried canaries but felt too touristy. Looking at Greek islands and Crete currently.
>
> Is there anywhere you like?

Hi Walter i am from Crete.. But i live in London...

The south of the island is not greatly populated. In August it can be packed though... Every summer it gets busier and busier... I would avoid the north of the island as it is more dense populated. You got the typical British / German / Scandinavian pockets ..
There lots of flights passing over and lots of military activity as well.. There is an American Military base in Chania next to the airport.. For many years people have been getting really sick from the villages around.. I think the south could be better but there are shipping routes passing from the Libyan sea underneath Crete so you will be getting their radar emission...There is another radar in the South West too.. no bueno..

I have thought about it many times to go back there ... and find an a quite spot and stay ..but i am not sure if i want to live there any more.. maybe because i am from there the spark is completely gone..

Unless you go down there with the gear and test first, you cant really say 100%, what i said above is my opinion based on what i have seen there.. my plan is to do it one day but i don't know how i will manage.. My knees are currently screwed big time so i cant easily roam the wild coastline in the south..Until i fix them..

If you want to know more, ask and i might be able to answer

### Inger — 2018-03-19T23:07:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/we-need-a-where-to-move-to-thread.19529/post-237558>

I was in Mollina, 500 meters high in the mountains 1 hour drive north of Malaga, southern Spain, for 3 week vacation at my sisters home. It rained all the time, it is very unusual! But still, despite the rain.... something there felt very good to me......
it was like going back.. many many years. There was a certain peace surrounding everything.

No Chemtrails.
Sun.. the few hours I got to see it.. was awesome! Clear and strong.
LOTS of birds! They were singing so loud, and all the time! Good sign to me.

I ate Iberico pork, fresh, all the time! It was pretty cheap too. Ribeye was another favorite. And the blood sausage was the best I have EVER eaten. Man, was that good.
And seafood was great and lots of nice stuff available. Eating healthy there is not an issue! I had a lot of mussles, and they were HUGE!
Lots of awesome sheep and goat raw cheese products. Just awesome. I ate lots of their local cheeses...yum!!

The water.... it was awesome!!! Not from the tap...lol. The tapwater was aweful, very chlorinated. But their bottled spring water was dirt cheap!!!! I got 6 x 1,5 liter great quality Sierra Nevada spring water (from altitude, 1000-1500 meters above sea level) for about 80 cent alltogether! Means less that 10 cent / liter I guess!
I still cannot believe how dirt cheap it was...... and I bet it was deuterium depleted too

I need to go back again in spring.... when the sun is out
I just... liked it

Michalis if you go a little up in the mountains in south Spain there will be very little tourists. I saw sooo much silent land and no turists at all. Beautiful moutains. Very remote. Very poor. On the road from Mollina to Malaga the view was stunningly beautiful. If I had a partner that is where I would camp and heal. As a lone woman it is probably not wise, IDK. But as a man you could easily do it!
There are wild horses too.... and wolfs.. and boar

Thanks Michalis. I was looking at Makry Gialos in the South. Any opinions much appreciated?

### Michalis — 2018-03-19T23:57:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/we-need-a-where-to-move-to-thread.19529/post-237567>

> [Neil Taylor said:](/goto/post?id=237563)
>
> Thanks Michalis. I was looking at Makry Gialos in the South. Any opinions much appreciated?
>
> Marine radar will be an issue throughout European islands I imagine. I’m just starting to look into it - I’d not considered it before but it has come up a number of times in this post. I previously lived 5 miles away from an airport and that was a nightmare.

I have passed from Makry Giallos once or twice but never stayed. (I am from the other side of the island) There is tourism in the summer but definitely not much compared with tourist hordes in the North of the island. Much much more mellow. The south east is generally more laid back and the climate is drier too.. The good thing is that you will be able to see the sunrise easier from there  There lots of nice beaches a stones through away towards the eastern bit of the island if you want peace and quiet. Renting a car is not a bad idea. Explore the south of the island as you might find something you like more.Driving is safe as long as you keep your distances and don't try to match the local cowboys, let them do their bit and don't bother..

I mean all these people are travelling to the states for a better future.. doesn't make sense.. **If you got money then you dont have to worry about it of course..**\[/quote\]
\-------------------------------------------------------------------
If you got money then you get platoon of body guards,
or
live in guarded community.

My story in Brazil.

...........
If you have large chunk of gold hanging off your neck on Copacabana beach, you get knife between ribs.
Luckily it was not me, but coworker was less lucky.

........

I agree.

Beware this is all very double edged if you pay close attention. Even on this site you will see young men wishing they had the optionality that assets afford them and you'll see older men with assets wishing they had the optionality that youth affords them.

Gawking at the optionality that you don't currently have without taking action is a trap. Stop looking at it and use the optionality and resources you do have.

I currently working a few of what we call side hustles here in the US. Instead of drinking that beer or watching TV at the end of the day or sleeping in in the morning you spend a small amount of your energy to try to float an idea out to the market and see if there are any "bites". Most of my efforts fail, but some have been profitable and I'm now in the middle of a big one that may pan out very nicely and be repeatable or it could be a wash (or worse).

I'm in my middle ages now so maybe I should be half envious of youth and half envious of more assets. Forget that, I'm too busy hustling my butt.

Check this out Antonis:
[https://www.artofmanliness.com/2017/10/03/podcast-344-art-side-hustle/](https://www.artofmanliness.com/2017/10/03/podcast-344-art-side-hustle/)

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/we-need-a-where-to-move-to-thread.19529/post-237778>

Planning for things to not work out I a move is pointless and hypothetical.

You meet people, opportunity arises in a variety of ways.

If you are retired and have a decent pension you can live in Yucatan CHEAP and be v optimal. Rent food and water is so cheap if u cook your own.

If you are younger it is harder. If you have a family it is harder than that!!

Personally, I'm staying here til my money runs out or I find a job, but ok I've found opportunities already I 1 week... Just have to minimize your expectations of pay and start thinking about long term ideas for income...

Don't hate the health/medical media; become the health media by speaking truths of nature online about public health issues that affect us all. Distribute natural laws yourself to the masses who are starving the truth. Today, readership is highly dependent upon format and distribution as much as it is on content. Where are you? That ignorance is a huge advantage to people like me. PEER review journals are old media in medicine. Their rules are no longer my rules. If you want to change the world you take that change directly to the masses with illness and sickness who are getting Rx’s over truth.
